    1. RoleView(RoleService, PermissionRecordService, DepartmentService)

Class RoleView

java.lang.Object
com.vaadin.flow.component.Component
com.vaadin.flow.component.splitlayout.SplitLayout
de.hallerweb.enterprise.prioritize.ui.security.RoleView
All Implemented Interfaces:
com.vaadin.flow.component.AttachNotifier, com.vaadin.flow.component.ClickNotifier<com.vaadin.flow.component.splitlayout.SplitLayout>, com.vaadin.flow.component.DetachNotifier, com.vaadin.flow.component.HasElement, com.vaadin.flow.component.HasSize, com.vaadin.flow.component.HasStyle, com.vaadin.flow.component.HasTheme, com.vaadin.flow.component.shared.HasThemeVariant<com.vaadin.flow.component.splitlayout.SplitLayoutVariant>, Serializable
@Route("roles") @PermitAll public class RoleView extends com.vaadin.flow.component.splitlayout.SplitLayout
Master-detail CRUD screen for Role, including the C/R/U/D permission matrix that is the point of this slice. The grid lists all roles; the side form edits a role's base fields (name, description) and its optional Department scope, and — for an already persisted role — its PermissionRecords as a checkbox matrix.

Same master-detail conventions as UserView/CompanyView (a SplitLayout with a draggable divider starting at 30/70, a placeholder while nothing is selected). Like

invalid reference 
UserService
and the backing RoleService/PermissionRecordService, this is an admin-only screen behind the login, so there are no per-call authorization parameters or AccessDeniedException handling.

Permission matrix. Each row is one PermissionRecord targeting one object type at the type level (objectId = 0, i.e. "all instances of this type"); the four Create/Read/Update/Delete checkboxes map 1:1 to the record's flags. Editing is immediate: toggling a checkbox, adding a target type or removing a row calls RoleService.addPermissionToRole(Long, PermissionRecord)/ RoleService.removePermissionFromRole(Long, Long)/PermissionRecordService.updatePermission(Long, PermissionRecord, Long) right away and reloads the matrix from the persisted role. The matrix therefore requires a saved role — while creating a new role it is disabled with a hint; save the base fields first, then select the role to manage its permissions.

Lazy-init guard (see CompanyView): base-field edits go through a fresh, detached Role bean carrying only name/description — never the entity returned by the finder — and the department is passed to the service by id, so RoleService.updateRole(Long, Role, Long) never touches a detached lazy relation. The role's own permissions and department are EAGER, so reading them off a finder result (for the grid and when loading the matrix) is safe.

Author:
peter haller
See Also:
